questions.q: The crescograph was an instrument designed to measure what in plants?
questions.explanation: The crescograph was built to measure and record the very small, slow growth of plants.

questions.q: Which Indian scientist invented the crescograph?
questions.explanation: The crescograph was invented by the polymath Jagadish Chandra Bose.

questions.q: What did the crescograph mainly do to a plant's tiny movements so they could be observed?
questions.explanation: The crescograph magnified the minute movements of plant growth enormously so they could be seen and recorded.

questions.q: The crescograph is associated with the study of which kingdom of living things?
questions.explanation: The crescograph was a tool of plant physiology, used to study how plants grow and respond.

questions.q: In what broad field of science is the crescograph an important early instrument?
questions.explanation: By applying physical measurement to living plants, the crescograph is a landmark of biophysics.

questions.q: Bose used the crescograph to argue that plants do what in response to their surroundings?
questions.explanation: Bose used the crescograph to show plants respond to stimuli such as light, temperature, and touch.

questions.q: Why was an instrument like the crescograph needed to study plant growth directly?
options: Plant growth is far too fast to see
Plant growth is normally too slow to see directly
Plants do not grow at all
questions.explanation: Ordinary plant growth is so slow that it cannot be seen directly, so the crescograph magnified it to make it visible.

questions.q: The word 'crescograph' relates to which idea?
questions.explanation: 'Cresco' comes from the Latin for 'to grow,' and '-graph' means 'to record,' so it is a growth recorder.

questions.q: In which country did Bose carry out his crescograph and plant-response research?
questions.explanation: Bose did his crescograph research in India, mainly in Calcutta (Kolkata).

questions.q: The crescograph helped record not only growth but also a plant's response to which everyday factor?
questions.explanation: Bose used the crescograph to record how plant growth changed in response to light, among other stimuli.

questions.q: In roughly which period did Bose develop and demonstrate the crescograph?
questions.explanation: Bose developed his plant-response instruments, including the crescograph, around the turn of the 20th century.

questions.q: Bose's high-magnification crescograph was reported to magnify plant movement by a factor on the order of which value?
options: About a million times or more
questions.explanation: Bose's most refined crescograph could magnify the minute growth movements of a plant on the order of a million times or more.

questions.q: How did the basic crescograph make slow plant growth visible to an observer?
options: By using a system of levers and a moving record
By weighing the plant on a balance
questions.explanation: The crescograph attached the plant tip to a magnifying lever system that traced its movement onto a moving recording surface.

questions.q: Bose used the crescograph and related instruments to claim that plants possess what animal-like feature?
options: Electrical signalling like a nervous system
questions.explanation: Bose argued plants show electrical responses resembling a nervous system, recording their reactions to stimuli.

questions.q: In which city did Bose later found a research institute where such plant studies continued?
questions.explanation: Bose founded the Bose Institute in Calcutta in 1917, where his plant-physiology research carried on.

questions.q: Bose presented his plant-response findings to which prestigious scientific body in London?
questions.explanation: Bose demonstrated and discussed his plant work before the Royal Society and other leading scientific institutions in London.

questions.q: Besides ordinary growth, the crescograph could be used to study the effect of which on plants?
questions.explanation: Bose used crescograph recordings to study how chemicals, drugs, and poisons altered plant growth and response.

questions.q: Bose's broader theme, supported by the crescograph, was the similarity in response between living tissue and what?
questions.explanation: Bose argued that plants, animal tissue, and even some non-living materials showed comparable response and fatigue curves.

questions.q: What did Bose generally do with his inventions, including instruments like the crescograph?
options: Was reluctant to patent and shared his work freely
Sold them to a single company
questions.explanation: Bose was famously reluctant to patent his inventions, preferring to share his discoveries openly.

questions.q: The crescograph belongs to a family of sensitive recording instruments Bose built to measure what in plants?
questions.explanation: Bose designed a suite of ultra-sensitive instruments to detect and record the tiny movements and electrical responses of plants.

questions.q: Bose received which honour from the British Crown during the era of his plant research?
questions.explanation: Bose was knighted in 1917, becoming Sir Jagadish Chandra Bose.

questions.q: Before his plant work, Bose was already famous as a pioneer of what other technology?
questions.explanation: Bose first gained fame as a pioneer of very short radio waves before turning to plant physiology and the crescograph.

questions.q: Bose advanced the crescograph idea into a version that recorded growth automatically. What is this refined instrument often called?
questions.explanation: Bose developed an extremely sensitive, high-magnification crescograph (sometimes described as a magnetic crescograph) that traced growth automatically.

questions.q: Crescograph records typically displayed plant growth as what, plotted against time?
options: A traced curve or series of marks
questions.explanation: The crescograph produced a graphical trace, a curve or series of marks, showing how the plant grew over time.

questions.q: Bose's crescograph experiments led him to report that growth could be momentarily affected by stimuli in what way?
options: Growth was completely unaffected by anything
Growth only occurred once per year
Growth could be sped up or slowed by stimulation
questions.explanation: Bose reported that stimuli such as electric shock, drugs, or temperature could temporarily accelerate or retard a plant's growth rate.

questions.q: Bose published influential books on plant response. Which subject best describes their focus?
options: Plant irritability and response to stimuli
questions.explanation: Bose authored works such as 'Plant Response' and 'Researches on Irritability of Plants' detailing his crescograph-based findings.

questions.q: A key principle behind the crescograph's enormous magnification was the use of which mechanical approach?
options: Compound levers and optical or mechanical amplification
questions.explanation: The crescograph used compounded levers and amplification to turn a microscopic growth increment into a large, recordable motion.

questions.q: Bose's claim that plants 'feel' and respond, supported by the crescograph, was received how by the scientific community of his time?
options: Awarded a Nobel Prize at once
questions.explanation: Bose's bold claims about plant sensitivity drew considerable skepticism and debate among contemporary scientists.

questions.q: The crescograph is best categorised as which kind of historical scientific object?
questions.explanation: The crescograph is a scientific instrument, an apparatus engineered to measure and record plant growth.

questions.q: Bose's crescograph work is celebrated today chiefly because it pioneered which broader idea?
options: That plants are made of metal
That plants do not need water
That plants can speak human languages
Applying precise physical measurement to living organisms
questions.explanation: The crescograph pioneered the use of precise physical instruments to quantify the behaviour of living organisms, a foundation of modern biophysics.
